identity.name: Stripe
identity.summary: Stripe is a financial-infrastructure platform that lets businesses accept payments and move money online via APIs.

sources.title: Stripe | Financial Infrastructure for the Internet
Stripe raises tender offer valuing it at ~$65B
Stripe processes over $1 trillion in payments
Adyen vs Stripe: payments market share
The Collison brothers and Stripe's founding
Stripe expands into stablecoins and billing
Fintech valuations and the road to IPO

bullets.text: Stripe provides payment-processing and financial infrastructure delivered as developer-first APIs, letting businesses accept cards and other methods with a few lines of code.
Its product suite has expanded well beyond checkout to include billing/subscriptions, Connect (marketplaces), issuing, invoicing, tax, and fraud tooling (Radar).
Founded in 2010 by Irish brothers Patrick and John Collison, the company is headquartered in San Francisco and Dublin.
Patrick Collison serves as CEO; his brother John Collison is President — the pair co-founded Stripe in 2010 and remain in control.
The company has recruited senior operators from Google, Meta, and Goldman Sachs across engineering, finance, and risk functions.
Its board and investor base include partners from Sequoia, General Catalyst, and Founders Fund who hold significant influence.
A 2024 employee tender offer valued the still-private company at roughly $65B, down from a 2021 peak of ~$95B but rebounding from 2023 lows.
Backers include Sequoia, Andreessen Horowitz, Founders Fund, and Thrive Capital across multiple mega-rounds.
Stripe has scaled to thousands of employees while serving millions of businesses from startups to large enterprises.
Stripe remains privately held — there is no public ticker or market cap; liquidity comes via periodic employee tender offers rather than a listing.
The company reported crossing $1 trillion in total payment volume processed in a single year, rivaling major card networks' merchant volume.
Revenue is driven by a small percentage take-rate on payment volume plus subscription fees on higher-margin software products like Billing and Tax.
Its closest large-scale rival is Adyen, which competes hard for enterprise merchants on take-rate and unified commerce.
PayPal (Braintree) and Block (Square) compete on different segments — checkout ubiquity and SMB/in-person respectively.
Stripe differentiates on developer experience and breadth of programmable money-movement products rather than lowest price.
Remaining private with no confirmed IPO timeline creates liquidity pressure for employees and early investors.
Payments is a thin-margin, high-competition business; take-rate compression from Adyen and in-housing by large merchants is a persistent threat.
Interchange and payments regulation across the US and EU could pressure economics.
Stripe has pushed into stablecoin-based money movement, signaling a bet on crypto rails for cross-border payments.
Continued expansion of its Billing and Tax products targets recurring-revenue software companies.
Secondary-market tender offers indicate the company is managing employee liquidity while staying private.

verdict.verdict: Stripe is the category-defining developer-first payments platform with trillion-dollar processing scale and an expanding financial-infrastructure stack. Its core strength is unmatched developer breadth; the single biggest risk is take-rate compression in a brutally competitive, thin-margin market while it remains private with no clear IPO path.
verdict.rationale: Findings are corroborated across multiple high-quality financial-press sources with consistent figures.
